EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front passed through on the 1st. A southwest flow ushered in warm and moist air ahead of the boundary. Showers and a few thunderstorms developed, and they were able to mix down strong winds from aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Branches and a few trees were down along 17th and H Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The northern and southern branches of the jet phased together, which resulted in coastal low pressure on the 13th. The coastal low tracked up the Mid-Atlantic Coast during the morning hours of the 14th before moving out to sea later in the day. High pressure over New England caused enough cold air for precipitation to start out as snow. However, warmer air did work its way in aloft causing precipitation to change to sleet and and freezing rain. This cut down on some of the snow totals, but ice accumulation from freezing rain also took place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 3.1 inches near the National Zoo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure was located across the Tennessee Valley while a strong low level jet transported moisture into the Mid-Atlantic. Showers and thunderstorms led to heavy rain across northern and central Virginia leading to scattered flooding mainly from rivers and creeks over flowing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 1200 Block of 16th Street was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passed through the area and plenty of cold air was in place for a period of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 1.1 inches near the National Zoo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cutoff low pressure was over the Ohio Valley. Another area of low pressure strengthened overhead on the occluded boundary. Warm and moist air from the south lead to an unstable atmosphere. Strong shear profiles were in place due to the cutoff low to the west. Strong lift associated with the developing low combined with strong shear and enough instability to produce severe thunderstorms and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Baltimore MD/Washington DC has |confirmed an EF-0 tornado initiating in Joint Base Anacostia Bolling |and continuing into Anacostia DC on April 6, 2017.  On the two mile |path, the weak tornado caused several small and midsized trees to be |snapped and uprooted on base in a convergent manner. Flags were |stripped off their posts in the central roundabout.  A large soccer |net was thrown from the athletic field. Shingle damage was noted to |one of the buildings. As the tornado crossed over Interstate 295 it |likely lifted off the ground. Trees were snapped higher up on the |trunks on the hill just east of the interstate. An apartment complex |was unroofed on Stanton Rd SE. Media reported one person was injured |by flying drywall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated afternoon and evening thunderstorms created flooding that intensified to flash flooding in Northwest Washington, DC during the evening of August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broad Branch was reported to be out of its banks and spilling over a couple inches deep onto Broad Branch Road in Northwest DC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated afternoon and evening thunderstorms created flooding that intensified to flash flooding in Northwest Washington, DC during the evening of August 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The earlier flooding intensified and water rapidly spilled onto Broad Branch Road between 27th Street and Grant Road. This water was deep enough to force a road closure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and an unstable atmosphere led to some severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down in the 1900 Block of 4th Street Northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed in the morning of August 15th, as a warm front was forced northward into the region, ahead of Hurricane Gert. The heavy rain produced flash flooding in Northwest DC during the midday hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water due to torrential rain reported in the 4500 block of Van Ness Street NW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed in the morning of August 15th, as a warm front was forced northward into the region, ahead of Hurricane Gert. The heavy rain produced flash flooding in Northwest DC during the midday hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broad Branch Road flooded and closed due to flash flooding on Broad Branch near Brandywine Street NW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southwesterly flow around high pressure over the Atlantic pumped in plenty of moisture while hot conditions persisted due to an upper-level ridge of high pressure. The heat and humidity caused dangerous heat indices.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices were around 105 degrees based on observations nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level trough and disturbance associated with the trough passed through the area on the 9th. The forcing from the disturbance caused a period of snow to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled up to 2.0 inches at Delcarlia Reservoir and one inch at the National Arboretum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed late in the afternoon of August 18th, primarily in the Broad Branch and Rock Creek stream basins northwest of Washington, DC. This heavy rain caused isolated flash flooding in Northwest DC during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain brought Broad Branch out of its banks, causing six to eight inches of water to flow across Broad Branch Road between 32nd Street NW and Brandywine Street NW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain developed late in the afternoon of August 18th, primarily in the Broad Branch and Rock Creek stream basins northwest of Washington, DC. This heavy rain caused isolated flash flooding in Northwest DC during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A van became trapped in high water caused by torrential rainfall near the intersection of Tilden Street NW and Beach Drive NW. This necessitated a water rescue.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rock Creek and Potomac Parkway flooded and closed near Connecticut Avenue NW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Canadian high pressure shifted overhead, causing temperatures to drop well below freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped well below freezing.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The waterspout then moved onshore near the George Mason Memorial |at the Tidal Basin at approximately 1:41 PM EDT. Here, multiple |large softwood and hardwood trees, including several ornamental |cherry trees, had large branches downed and/or were uprooted |around the Tidal Basin. While some minor tree damage was evidenced|near the George Mason Memorial on the southwest side of the Tidal|Basin, the most concentrated damage was in the grove of trees |directly adjacent to the parking lot of the Tidal Basin Marina.||The last damage reported to the NWS was to the roof of the St. |Aloysius Church at the intersection of North Capitol and I |Streets NW at 145 PM EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure both at the surface and aloft caused hot conditions. A southerly flow caused high humidity, which led to high heat indices.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat indices around 105 degrees were reported at Reagan National.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low interacted with a frontal boundary near the Mid-Atlantic region and low pressure formed along the boundary. High moisture content and thunderstorms led to widespread flooding across the Mid-Atlantic region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gauge on Rock Creek at Rock Creek/Sherrill Drive exceeded the flood stage of 7 feet. It peaked at 8.36 feet at 23:30 EST. Water reached several portions of the Valley Trail between Picnic Areas 7 and 10 in Rock Creek Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passed through the area. At the same time, an upper-level trough passed through increasing the winds aloft. There was enough shear to combine with an unstable atmosphere for severe thunderstorms to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down along Jocelyn Street Northwest and another tree fell onto a house in the 5300 Block of 32nd Street Northwest.
